revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:服部天神宮]]
{{Infobox religious building | name = Hattori Tenjin Shrine | native_name = 服部天神宮 | native_name_lang = | image = File:服部天神宮 社殿.jpg | image_upright = | alt = | caption = 服部天神宮 社殿 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | tradition = | sect = | district = | prefecture = {{ill|Toyonaka|qid=Q467479|1=Toyonaka}} | province = | region = | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|qid=Q3275687|1=Sukunabikona}} | rite = | festivals= {{Ublist| Toyonaka Ebisu festival | Great festival for the protection of the feet }} | organisational_status = | ownership = | governing_body = | leadership = | bhattaraka = | patron = | consecration_year = | functional_status = | religious_features_label = | religious_features = | location = Hattori Motomachi 1-2-17, {{ill|Toyonaka|en|Toyonaka|qid=Q467479|lt=Toyonaka|1=Toyonaka}} City, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}} | locale = | municipality = | cercle = | state = | country = Japan | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | grid_name = | grid_position = | sector = | territory = | administration = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.763028|N|135.475972|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | coordinates_footnotes = | heritage_designation = | architect = | architecture_type = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | creator = | funded_by = | general_contractor = | established = | groundbreaking = | year_completed = | construction_cost = | date_demolished = | facade_direction = | capacity = | length = | width = | width_nave = | interior_area = | height_max = | dome_quantity = | dome_height_outer = | dome_height_inner = | dome_dia_outer = | dome_dia_inner = | minaret_quantity = | minaret_height = | spire_quantity = | spire_height = | site_area = | temple_quantity = | monument_quantity = | shrine_quantity = | inscriptions = | materials = | elevation_m = | elevation_footnotes = | nrhp = | designated = | a11ed = | refnum = | delisted1_date = | website = {{URL|1=https://hattoritenjingu.or.jp/gosanpai?lang=en}} | module = | footnotes = | map = Osaka city | founded = during the reign of [[Emperor Ingyo]] }}
'''Hattori Tenjingu Shrine''' is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Hattori Motomachi, {{ill|Toyonaka|en|Toyonaka|qid=Q467479|lt=Toyonaka|1=Toyonaka}} city, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, Japan where it is believed leg ailments can be healed.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Hattori Tenjingu |url=https://hattoritenjingu.or.jp/gosanpai?lang=en |access-date=2023-04-27 |website=hattoritenjingu.or.jp}}</ref> Its former Rank was village shrine or son-sha. In the Kansai region, it is known as the "god of the feet."
== deity == * Main deity - [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]], {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|qid=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}
== History == {{Right| [[File:Toyonaka-Ebisu H19.jpg|thumb|240px|Toyonaka Ebisu festival (2007)]] [[File:Hattori-tenjingu Minazuki-O-harae.jpg|thumb|240px|June [[Ōharae-shiki]] (passing through a straw ring)]] [[File:Hattori-tenjingu Ashi-no-shugo.jpg|thumb|240px|Great festival to pray for the protection of the feet (2006)]] }} The exact period of the shrine's establishment is unclear, but it is believed that the {{ill|Hata clan|en|Hata clan|qid=Q987290|lt=Hata clan|1=Hata clan}}, an {{ill|Toraijin|ja|帰化人|en|lt=immigrant clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toraijin|1=kikajin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11481249}}, enshrined the foreign god Sukunahikona no Mikoto (the god of medicine) when they were appointed as Weavers during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|qid=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyo|1=Emperor Ingyō}} (412 - 453) and made the area their base. At that time, it was still a small shrine. However, it was not the Hata clan but the Mara no Sukune of the {{ill|Izu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊豆国造|1=Isu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11380553}} clan who were granted the surname Hattori during the reign of Emperor Ingyo<ref>"Yamato Province, Kami-betsu, Hattori no Muraji" '{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=}}'.</ref><ref>{{ill|Matoshi Suzuki|ja|鈴木眞年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matoshi Suzuki|qq=}} "Izu no Sukune Genealogy" 'Hundred Family Genealogies'. 「伊豆宿禰系図」『百家系図稿』一巻。</ref>, and there is also a theory that their [[Oyagami]] was Sukunahikona no Mikoto<ref>"[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keijiban/titibu1.htm Hei Sei Chichibu Clan and Dan Party]" 'Ancient Tree Chronicle Room', 2004.</ref>. The shrine dedicated to Sukunahikona no Mikoto later became known as a Tenjin Shrine.
In {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|qid=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} 2 (783), {{ill|Fujiwara no Uona|en|Fujiwara no Uona|qid=Q385008|lt=Fujiwara no Uona|1=Fujiwara no Uona}} was demoted to {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|qid=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu|1=Dazaifu (government)}} and headed to {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|qid=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province|1=Chikuzen Province}}, but he fell ill and died in the area of Kawabe-so, which he owned, and was buried near Tenjin Shrine. His tomb, known as "Kawabe Sadaijin Fujiwara no Uona Ko no Haka," still remains in the shrine grounds. About 100 years later, in {{ill|Engi (era)|en|Engi (era)|qid=Q1374325|lt=Engi|1=Engi (era)}} 1 (901), {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q382005}} was similarly demoted to {{ill|Dazai Gon no Sochi|ja|大宰権帥|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dazai Gon no Sochi|1=Dazai-no-gon-no-sochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q9891637}} and, while en route to his post, was stricken with {{ill|Thiamine deficiency|en|Thiamine deficiency|qid=Q184084|lt=beriberi|1=Thiamine deficiency}} and could not move. Following the advice of the local people, he prayed for recovery at the roadside Tenjin Shrine and the five-storied pagoda dedicated to Uona, and miraculously regained his health, allowing him to reach his post.
After Sugawara no Michizane's death, the rising faith in Tenjin led to his enshrinement at the shrine, and new buildings were constructed. From this time, the shrine became known as "Hattori Tenjingu" and was revered as the "Foot God" due to Sugawara no Michizane's story.
In the early modern period, the area developed into a thriving town due to its location on the {{ill|Nose Highway|ja|能勢街道|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nose Highway|qq=}}, and during the {{ill|Bunka|en|Bunka|qid=Q1373146|lt=Bunka period|1=Bunka}} (1804 - 1817) and {{ill|Bunsei|en|Bunsei|qid=Q1327119|lt=Bunsei|1=Bunsei}} (1818 - 1829) periods in the late {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it reached its peak of prosperity.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} era, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Son-sha|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Son-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. On March 10, 1910 (Meiji 43), the Minou Electric Railway (now {{ill|Hankyu|en|Hankyu|qid=Q473058|lt=Hankyu Railway|1=Hankyu}}) opened, and Hattori Station was established. On December 21, 2013 (Heisei 25), Hattori Station on the {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚線|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阪急宝塚本線|1=Hankyu Takarazuka Main Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3832859}} was renamed {{ill|Hattori-tenjin Station|en|Hattori-tenjin Station|qid=Q3969777|lt=Hattori Tenjin Station|1=Hattori-tenjin Station}}. This name change was part of Hankyu Railway's policy to "make historical tourist attractions near the station easy to understand."
The shrine is worshipped not only by those praying for the healing of foot ailments but also by athletes such as {{ill|Association football|en|Association football|qid=Q2736|lt=soccer|1=Association football}} and {{ill|Marathon|en|Marathon|qid=Q40244|lt=marathon|1=Marathon}} participants who primarily use their feet.
== Subshrines == Toyonaka Ebisu Shrine is an ebisu shrine originally located on the residence of Shinjirō Torii ([[:ja:鳥井信治郎|鳥井 信治郎]], ''Torii Shinjirō'') the founder of {{ill|Suntory|en|Suntory|qid=Q1345267|lt=Suntory|1=Suntory}}
== References == {{reflist}}
* 服部天神宮社務所「『足の神様・服部天神宮』由緒」 * {{Cite book |editor=豊中市史編さん委員会 |title=新修豊中市史 |volume=第6巻（美術） |publisher=豊中市 |date=December 2005 |id={{JPNO|20998637}} }} * {{Cite book |editor=豊中市史編さん委員会 |title=新修豊中市史 |volume=第9巻（集落・都市） |publisher=豊中市 |date=March 1998 |id={{JPNO|99031344}} }} * {{Cite book |author=西田王堂 |title=西田王堂作品集 |publisher=産経新聞社 |date=November 1988 |pmid=BB12453637 }} * {{Cite book |author=矢野橋村 |editor=望月信成 |title=矢野橋村名作選集 |publisher=清文堂出版 |year=1975 |id={{JPNO|75041292}} }} * {{Cite book |author=八谷正 |title=安田青風の人と作品 |publisher=短歌友の会連盟 |date=November 1970 |pmid=BA57173534 }}
==External links== *{{Official website|1=https://hattoritenjingu.or.jp/gosanpai?lang=en|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine official website}} *[https://www.tripadvisor.com/Attraction_Review-g1023472-d7655931-Reviews-Hattori_Tenjingu_Shrine-Toyonaka_Osaka_Prefecture_Kinki.html Review at TripAdvisor] * [http://toyonaka-ebisu.com/ 豊中えびす] {{Commonscat|Hattori Tenjin-gū}}
}}
}
{{translated page|ja|服部天神宮|version=104645915|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:服部天神宮]]
